name: San Juan River
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding along the scenic San Juan River in southeastern Utah. Find colorful agates, jasper, and chalcedony in riverbed gravels and canyon walls.
description:
The San Juan River cuts through the high desert plateau of southeastern Utah, creating an accessible rockhounding destination for collectors of all skill levels. This stretch of BLM-managed river corridor offers excellent opportunities to find banded agates, red and brown jasper, and chalcedony nodules in the active riverbed and along eroded canyon walls. The river has carved through multiple geological formations, exposing colorful stones that have been tumbled and polished by millions of years of water flow.

The area is characterized by sandy beaches, gravel bars, and accessible terraces along the river. Collections are typically found in concentrated areas where the current has sorted heavier stones. The geology here reflects the San Juan River's role as a major drainage system through the Colorado Plateau, carrying sediment from the mountains to the north and depositing it in beds that contrast beautifully with the tan and red Entrada and Navajo sandstone walls.

What makes this location special is the combination of free access, minimal regulations, and genuine geological diversity. The river environment constantly refreshes collecting areas with new material, especially after seasonal flows. Visitors enjoy scenic canyon views while searching for specimens, making this as much a hiking and photography destination as a rockhounding site.

Best collecting happens by wading in shallow areas and carefully examining gravel bars. The river is relatively gentle in most spots, making it safer than many Utah waterways. Always check water levels before visiting, as spring runoff can make wading dangerous.
history: The San Juan River is a major tributary of the Colorado River, carved through the northern Colorado Plateau over millions of years. As the river eroded through Jurassic and Triassic rock formations deposited during ancient seas and deserts, it exposed colorful agate and jasper layers formed from silica-rich groundwater precipitation. Local prospectors and collectors have worked this river for decades, and the area was historically important for uranium exploration during the Cold War mining boom, though most modern activity is recreational rockhounding.
tips: Focus your search on gravel bars and the active riverbed after the water level drops - agates and jasper concentrate on inside curves where the current slows. Use a small classifier or mesh screen to sift gravels efficiently; bring a 5-gallon bucket to avoid hitting your daily 25-pound limit too quickly. Look for banded patterns and waxy luster in semi-translucent stones - these indicate quality agate rather than common quartz. The best specimens are often found half-buried in sand; brush and examine stones carefully rather than just surface collecting.
terrain_notes: The San Juan River corridor consists of sandy beaches, gravel bars, and slickrock terraces. Elevation ranges from 4,000 to 4,200 feet. Terrain is relatively flat near the water but slopes upward to canyon walls. Vegetation is sparse - desert shrubs, cottonwoods near water. Walking distances from parking areas to productive collecting spots range from 50 feet to half a mile. Footing is sandy to rocky, generally stable but can be uneven on gravel bars.
best_season: March - May and September - November. Spring offers good water levels and comfortable temperatures, while fall provides cooler collecting weather. Summer heat (100+ degrees F) makes midday collecting unpleasant. Winter is possible but water levels may be low and temperatures can drop into freezing range at night.
written_directions: From Bluff, Utah, drive south on US Route 191 for approximately 8 miles. Watch for pullouts and access points along the river on the west side of the highway. The most accessible area is near the Goseneck viewpoint turnoff. Turn west onto the marked BLM access road and follow it to river-level parking areas. Several informal pullouts and small parking areas line the river corridor along US 191.
road_conditions: US Route 191 is paved. BLM access roads are unpaved but passable in passenger vehicles during dry conditions. Some minor washboard surface.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts and small parking areas along US Route 191 at river access points. No developed lot; parking is on unpaved shoulders or small cleared areas. Capacity varies from 2-5 vehicles per pullout. No amenities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or equipment. Respect private property boundaries. Leave no trash. Pack out all waste.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No trash left behind
Respect adjacent private property
nearest_services: Bluff, Utah is approximately 8 miles north via US Route 191. Supplies, gas, food, and lodging available in Bluff. Smaller facilities in Mexican Hat, 20 miles south.
accessibility_notes: Easy terrain suitable for most ages and fitness levels. Relatively flat or gentle slopes near the water. Sandy and gravel footing is manageable for most hikers with sturdy footwear. Wading in shallow water is optional. No wheelchair access to riverbed, but road viewpoints are accessible. Short walks to productive areas make this friendly for families and older collectors.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding along the San Juan River near Bluff, Utah?
faq.answer: The San Juan River BLM area is known for banded agates, red and brown jasper, chalcedony nodules, and quartz specimens. Agates are the most prized find here, often showing beautiful banding and semi-translucent quality. These form naturally in the river gravels from silica deposits weathered out of the surrounding canyon walls.

faq.question: Is there a permit or fee required to rockhound the San Juan River in Utah?
faq.answer: No permit or fee is required for recreational rockhounding on this BLM land. Collection is completely free under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). You are limited to 25 pounds per day per person for personal use only - commercial collection is not allowed.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound near Bluff on the San Juan River?
faq.answer: The best seasons are March through May and September through November. Spring offers moderate temperatures and decent water levels after snowmelt. Fall is cooler and pleasant. Summer temperatures often exceed 100 degrees F, and winter water levels can be too low. Avoid heavy spring runoff when the river is dangerous.

faq.question: Is the San Juan River rockhounding site beginner-friendly and safe for families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, the San Juan River is excellent for beginners and families. Terrain near the water is relatively flat and easy to navigate. Kids as young as 6 can participate, though older children have easier time with collecting. Watch for flash flood risk during storms and be cautious of snakes in warm months. The area is generally safe when common desert precautions are taken.

faq.question: What tools and equipment should you bring for rockhounding the San Juan River?
faq.answer: Bring a 5-gallon bucket to stay within your 25-pound daily limit, a small classifier or mesh screen to sift gravels efficiently, a hand magnifier to examine specimens, sturdy boots for sandy and rocky terrain, a backpack with plenty of water, sun protection, and a light pick or chisel. A GPS or smartphone map is helpful for locating access points along US Route 191.

faq.question: How far is the San Juan River rockhounding area from Bluff, Utah and what roads do you take?
faq.answer: The San Juan River BLM access points are about 8 miles south of Bluff via US Route 191. The drive takes roughly 15 minutes. Simply follow US 191 south from Bluff and watch for pullouts and informal parking areas along the river on the west side of the highway. The Goseneck viewpoint area marks a major access zone. No special roads - it is all paved highway access to parking areas.

meta_title: San Juan River Rockhounding - Utah BLM
meta_description: Free rockhounding on BLM land near Bluff, Utah. Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony along the scenic San Juan River. No permit required.
